ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean marinated beef short ribs (Galbi). The closest parent term for beef rib food product is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef meat, ribs) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Korean marinated galbi preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Korean marinated beef short ribs. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (beef kalbi with rice and side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Beef Kalbi consists of approximately 400 g, comprising marinated grilled beef short ribs (approximately 200 g), steamed white rice (approximately 150 g), and assorted Korean side dishes / banchan (approximately 50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Beef, short ribs, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 13352); supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods data for marinated Korean beef rib preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked braised beef short ribs (NDB 13352 / FDC ID 174032). Adjustments applied for Korean marinade components (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, pear) based on USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. The dish is served with rice and banchan; per-100g values reflect the beef kalbi component only, as the composite dish nutrient profile is estimated. Sodium is elevated relative to plain braised ribs due to soy-sauce-based marinade. Sugar is elevated due to sugar and fruit in marinade. Values represent a reasonable evidence-based estimate for the marinated, wok-tossed preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on marinated beef with soy sauce and sugar marinade components
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean Beef Kalbi (Galbi). GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on the following reasoning: (1) The beef component itself has a negligible GI as a protein-fat food. (2) The marinade contains sugar and fruit juice, which contribute fermentable carbohydrates; however, the total carbohydrate per 100g is low (~5.8g). (3) The composite dish GI is dominated by the rice accompaniment, but per-100g values here reflect the kalbi component only. (4) Analogous reference: soy-sauce-marinated beef dishes from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables indicate low GI for protein-dominant marinated meat dishes. GI of 45 is a conservative low-category estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 30.0) / 100 = 13.5, where carbohydrate contribution from the kalbi portion of a 400g serving (200g kalbi × 5.8g carb/100g = 11.6g carb from kalbi) plus rice contribution (~18.4g net carb from 150g rice at ~12.3g/100g cooked) gives approximately 30g total carbohydrate per full serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce-based marinade (~520 mg sodium per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Beef Kalbi due to its high sodium content from the soy-sauce marinade; a single restaurant serving may contribute 1,000–1,500 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 1,500–2,000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content from marinade and moderate glycemic load when served with rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Beef Kalbi in controlled portions, noting that the marinade contributes approximately 4.2g sugar per 100g and the full meal with rice carries a moderate glycemic load (~13.5 per serving); pairing with lower-GI side dishes and reducing rice portion can improve postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6.8g/100g) and cholesterol (~72mg/100g) from beef short ribs, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Beef Kalbi consumption due to its saturated fat and cholesterol content from beef short ribs and its high sodium load from the marinade, both of which are associated with elevated cardiovascular risk; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Kalbi (갈비, romanized: Galbi) is a classic Korean dish consisting of beef short ribs that are marinated in a savory-sweet sauce typically made from soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and grated pear or apple, which acts as a natural meat tenderizer. The marinated ribs are traditionally grilled over charcoal or a gas grill, though restaurant preparations may also use a wok for caramelization and smoky flavour development. The result is tender, juicy beef with a deeply caramelized exterior, a balance of sweet and umami flavours, and a slightly smoky aroma. In Singapore, Beef Kalbi is served at Korean restaurants as a main course, typically accompanied by steamed white rice and an assortment of Korean side dishes (banchan) such as kimchi, pickled vegetables, and seasoned spinach. Nutritionally, the beef short rib component is rich in protein (~18.5g/100g), fat (~16.2g/100g including ~6.8g saturated fat), and provides significant zinc, vitamin B12, and iron. The marinade elevates sodium (~520mg/100g) and sugar (~4.2g/100g) content relative to plain cooked beef. The glycemic index of the kalbi component alone is estimated at 45 (low), though the full meal with rice carries a moderate glycemic load. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension due to high sodium, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol. Diabetic individuals should monitor portion size and rice quantity. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy or sesame allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Beef Kalbi?
Is Beef Kalbi high in sodium?
What is the GI of Korean BBQ beef?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked braised beef short ribs with adjustments for Korean marinade components. No exact match for Korean marinated galbi exists in a primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). The adaptation is evidence-based and defensible but not an exact source match. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174032, beef short ribs cooked braised) with marinade adjustments; no exact entry for Korean marinated galbi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of the access date. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this cut and preparation. 3. Per-100g values reflect the beef kalbi component only, not the full composite meal with rice and banchan. 4. Sodium is elevated relative to plain braised beef due to soy-sauce-based marinade; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly by recipe. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Korean Beef Kalbi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date. 6. Halal status is listed as false as standard Korean soy sauce and some marinade components may not be halal-certified; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for the beef rib component and are not independently verified for the marinated preparation. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for beef short ribs cooked braised.
